variables.content: Let $E_{\\mathrm{DMC}}(\\infty)$ denote the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit DMC energy per electron and let the quoted uncertainty accompanying $E_{\\mathrm{DMC}}(\\infty)$ be the reported one-standard-deviation error. The proposition is that the reported uncertainties for $E_{\\mathrm{DMC}}(\\infty)$ comprehensively encompass the stochastic Monte Carlo sampling error together with the significant systematic uncertainties associated with the finite-size extrapolation model choice (the $1/N$ ansatz), any residual time-step bias for the selected time steps, potential fixed-node/phase errors of the SJB trial functions, and other methodological approximations, so that the quoted error bars represent the total uncertainty in the thermodynamic-limit energy estimates.

For diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) simulations of a homogeneous Bose gas carried out in a periodic cubic simulation box with $N$ particles at number density $n$, the finite-size error decreases with increasing $N$; the proposition used in the numerical analysis is that for the DMC calculations in question performed with $N=500$ particles and the densities sampled in those calculations (number densities corresponding to $na^{3}$ values as small as $10^{-6}$ and up to $O(10^{-1})$), finite-size errors are small compared to the quoted statistical errors, so that the published DMC results can be treated as representative of the thermodynamic limit within the reported error bars; if this proposition fails (i.e., finite-size effects comparable to statistical errors), then diagnostics extracted from the DMC data would be biased.

When extrapolating MPC-corrected finite-size DMC energies to the infinite-cell limit by fitting $E_N^{\mathrm{cor}} = E_{\infty}^{\mathrm{cor}} + k^{\mathrm{cor}}/N$, the authors assign the uncertainty of the extrapolated infinite-size energy $E_{\infty}^{\mathrm{cor}}$ to be equal to the statistical uncertainty of the largest-supercell ($N=120$) DMC energy. The proposition relied upon is: this practice provides a conservative and adequate estimate of the true uncertainty in $E_{\infty}^{\mathrm{cor}}$, in the sense that it does not significantly underestimate the uncertainty introduced by the finite set of system sizes and the linear-in-$1/N$ extrapolation model and thus yields realistic one-standard-deviation error bars for downstream thermodynamic quantities.

Denote by $\sigma_{\mathrm{MC}}$ the statistical standard error from Monte Carlo sampling in a fixed‑phase DMC run for per‑particle energies and by $\sigma_{\mathrm{extrap}}$ the uncertainty arising from the finite‑size correction and extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit; for the DMC calculations carried out (system sizes and sampling lengths used), $\sigma_{\mathrm{MC}}\ll\sigma_{\mathrm{extrap}}$, so that the quoted error bars and the uncertainty budget are dominated by extrapolation systematics rather than by Monte Carlo statistical noise.

For the homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas at the densities studied, performing FN-DMC calculations for closed-shell particle numbers N in the range 21 ≤ N ≤ 126 (with N↑ and N↓ chosen to give closed-shell plane-wave fillings) provides sufficient sampling of shell-structure oscillations and finite-size trends so that the finite-N energy data can be fitted to the finite-size ansatz given above and extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit N→∞ with the claimed numerical precision.

For the interacting two-dimensional electron gas at the densities studied, fixed-node DMC energies were computed for particle numbers $N$ up to $N_{\\max}=126$ and down to $N_{\\min}=21$, and the proposition is that extrapolating these finite-$N$ results via the global finite-$N$/polarization fitting form yields reliable estimates of the thermodynamic-limit energy $E_{\\infty}(\\zeta)$, i.e., that finite-size effects not captured by the fit are small for $N\\le 126$ and do not qualitatively change the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit energies or the phase-stability conclusions derived from them.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

The computed quasiparticle effective mass m* for paramagnetic 2D-UEL exhibits a systematic dependence on the simulation-cell electron number N that is empirically well described by the form m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2} for some constant β; applying this N^{-3/2} empirical scaling to finite-N VMC and DMC data (for example N = 146, 218, 302) permits controlled extrapolation to estimates of m*(∞) in the thermodynamic limit. Observationally in the reported datasets, finite-size errors of m* are smaller in VMC than in DMC and the finite-size errors grow as the density is reduced (larger r_s).

Let E(N) denote diffusion Monte Carlo total energies per particle computed in periodic one-dimensional cells containing N electrons at fixed density. The proposition is that the simple two-parameter finite-size model E(N)=E_{\infty}+B N^{-2} (with E_{\infty} and B treated as N-independent constants over the fitted range) provides an adequate representation of the N-dependence of the DMC energies computed at N=37, 55, 73, and 99, so that a linear fit of E(N) versus 1/N^{2} over those four system sizes yields an accurate estimate of the thermodynamic-limit energy E_{\infty} with residual subleading corrections (higher powers of 1/N or nonanalytic finite-size terms) sufficiently small to be neglected within the stated uncertainties.

Twist-averaged diffusion Monte Carlo (TA DMC) total energies per electron E(N) computed for the three-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (HEG) in face-centered-cubic simulation cells with Ewald interactions at particle counts N = 130, 226, and 338 were extrapolated to infinite system size by fitting the linear finite-size model E(N) = E(∞) + b/N (where E(∞) is the thermodynamic-limit energy per electron and b is a scalar coefficient), and the resulting thermodynamic-limit DMC total energies per electron include, for r_s = 0.5, E_DMC(∞) = 3.4254(1) Ha/elec. (this dataset) compared with the prior DMC value 3.4301(1) Ha/elec. (Spink et al.), and for r_s = 1.0, E_DMC(∞) = 0.58640(2) Ha/elec. (this dataset) compared with 0.58780(1) Ha/elec. (Spink et al.); the extrapolation employed numerical 1/N scaling motivated by analytic finite-size considerations but did not apply analytic finite-size correction formulas directly.
